title: Initialization accepts failed oracle data and can seed an invalid exchange-rate cache
claim: `init()` ignores the `success` flag from `oracle.get()` and caches whatever rate is returned, including `0` or stale garbage. If the market is initialized while the oracle has no valid price, later failed `updateExchangeRate()` calls keep reusing that poisoned cache for solvency checks and liquidations.
impact: A Cauldron can start life with a zero or severely understated exchange rate, making bad debt appear solvent. Borrowers can then overborrow or remove too much collateral up to the available MIM / borrow cap until a successful oracle update finally replaces the cached rate, leaving the market with bad debt or undercollateralized positions.
paths: Initialize the clone while `oracle.get(oracleData)` returns `(false, 0)` or another invalid quote.
Let later `updateExchangeRate()` calls keep returning `success = false`, so the cached initialization value remains active.
Call `borrow()` or `cook(... ACTION_BORROW / ACTION_REMOVE_COLLATERAL ...)`; `_isSolvent()` uses the poisoned cached rate and allows positions that should fail solvency checks.

title: Oracle failures silently freeze solvency and liquidation logic on stale cached prices
claim: When `oracle.get()` returns `success = false`, `updateExchangeRate()` does not revert or block sensitive state transitions; it just reuses the last cached `exchangeRate`. Borrowing, collateral removal, and liquidation therefore continue against a frozen price even after the oracle itself says no valid current rate is available.
impact: During oracle outages or invalid-data windows, stale-low cached rates can let attackers overborrow or withdraw too much collateral, while stale-high cached rates can trigger liquidations that should not happen. The result can be bad debt, blocked liquidations, or unfair liquidations during periods when the oracle is explicitly signaling unusable data.
paths: Let the Cauldron cache a favorable exchange rate while the oracle is healthy.
Wait until the oracle starts returning `success = false` because its price is unavailable or too stale.
Use `borrow()`, `removeCollateral()`, `cook()`, or `liquidate()` while the contract keeps trusting the old cached rate instead of requiring a fresh valid oracle value.

title: The `cook` oracle upper-bound check is inverted
claim: `ACTION_UPDATE_EXCHANGE_RATE` enforces `(maxRate == 0 || rate > maxRate)` instead of requiring the fetched rate to stay at or below `maxRate`. Any nonzero upper bound therefore rejects normal prices and only passes when the oracle price is already above the caller's stated ceiling.
impact: The advertised upper-bound slippage guard is broken. Integrations and users batching oracle refreshes with borrows or collateral withdrawals cannot rely on `maxRate` to protect them from adverse oracle spikes, so flows that appear protected can still execute at prices above the intended ceiling.
paths: Build a `cook()` sequence containing `ACTION_UPDATE_EXCHANGE_RATE` with a nonzero `maxRate`.
Observe that acceptable rates revert, while rates above the supposed maximum pass the check.
Exploit the missing upper-bound protection in a batched borrow / collateral-withdraw flow during an oracle spike or manipulation event.

title: Permissionless `cook` can force collateral-strategy rebalances on deployments where the BentoBox owner hook is enabled
claim: `cook()` exposes `ACTION_RELEASE_COLLATERAL_FROM_STRATEGY` to every caller and directly invokes the BentoBox owner rebalance hook to set the collateral strategy target to `0`, then restores the prior target later in the batch. If the deployed BentoBox owner contract authorizes Cauldron to call this hook, any user can force a full exit and re-entry of the shared collateral strategy.
impact: Repeated permissionless strategy unwinds can grief the entire market by realizing withdrawal fees, slippage, harvest penalties, or operational rate-limit costs on the shared collateral pool. That can burn yield or realize losses for all users even though the attacker has no privileged role.
paths: Force the collateral strategy target to `0` and rebalance out, then let the function restore the old target at the end of the batch.
Repeat the sequence to repeatedly unwind and re-enter the shared strategy, accumulating any associated costs or disruption.

title: Interest-rate updates retroactively reprice already elapsed debt
claim: `changeInterestRate()` mutates `accrueInfo.INTEREST_PER_SECOND` without first calling `accrue()`. The next accrual therefore applies the new rate to the entire interval since `lastAccrued`, instead of only to time after the update.
impact: Elapsed debt can be retroactively overcharged or undercharged. A rate increase can suddenly push otherwise healthy borrowers into liquidation, while a rate decrease can forgive already-earned interest and create protocol accounting shortfalls.
paths: Let time pass without any function calling `accrue()`.
Trigger any later path that calls `accrue()`; the full elapsed period is charged at `newInterestRate` rather than the old rate up to the update timestamp.
